summary: Julia Evans announces a three-month sabbatical from Stripe to build better Ruby profiling tools through the Segment Open Source Fellowship. She's frustrated that Ruby and Python profiling requires choosing libraries, adding gems, and writing setup code, unlike C and Java where you can just attach to a running process. Her goal is to build a tool where you simply point at a PID and get useful profiling data immediately. She acknowledges this is new territory for her—she's used many profilers but never built one—and is both excited and nervous about the challenge.
key_points: Ruby and Python profiling tools require too much setup compared to C and Java, where you can attach to any running process instantly
The usability of profiling tools matters as much as their capability—tools people give up on aren't useful at all
Three core debugging capabilities should be easy to get from any program: stack traces, memory profiles, and CPU profiles/flamegraphs
Open source sabbaticals are a valuable model—you get to work on something meaningful and return to your job afterward
money_quotes: Profiling tools are not useful if they are so confusing to use that people give up!
There's so much low-hanging fruit in performance – maybe you accidentally wrote a quadratic function, maybe you wrote a small hacky thing and someone just needs to spend 30 minutes optimizing it.
Without a profiler, it's basically impossible to diagnose these performance problems!

I've often had a performance problem, thought 'oh, let me just get a memory profile of this node.js program, that will help', and 3 hours of frustration later been unable to get the information I wanted and given up.
I've never tried to make an open source project that other people actually use!
Anyway, how am I going to learn & get better if I don't do things that are a bit scary?
key_insight: The biggest barrier to fixing performance problems isn't the complexity of the fix—it's that profiling tools are too hard to set up, so people never get the data they need to diagnose the problem.
